text: The surgeon uses a flange retractor to effectively keep the bowels away from the surgical field in an obese patient.
The surgeon dissects the uterovascular fold, opening the anterior space, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgical view shows the anatomical space between the vagina and the bladder, likely highlighting the area for dissection or repair.
The surgeon dissects the space until reaching the trigone, the upper part of the bladder neck, or the start of the urethra, carefully navigating these anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon identifies the bladder by snugly fitting the Foley catheter bulb inside it, ensuring proper placement within the bladder cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the space between the vagina and bladder, demonstrating a rapid and bloodless separation of tissue planes.
The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the bladder trigone, marking the lower limit of the dissection area.
The surgeon elevates the bladder, utilizing the cavitational effect of carbon dioxide to expand the surgical space, facilitating easier laparoscopic access.
The surgeon utilizes a three-dimensional camera to enhance visualization during deep dissection, providing a detailed view of the anatomical structures involved in the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon ligates and anchors the pre-cut mesh in the anterior space, securing it in place as part of the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon places two anchor stitches along the vaginal wall to secure the mesh at the junction between the vagina, cervix, and bladder.
The surgeon uses esterless 3-0 non-absorbable sutures to anchor the mesh securely in place within the surgical field.
After completing the primary anchoring, the surgeon places the next anchor stitch slightly lower to stabilize the mesh and prevent wobbling within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the second anchor stitch and proceeds to place two additional anchor stitches at a specified level, securing the mesh in position.
The surgeon anchors the mesh at the apex of the cervix or at the level of the pubocervical ligament, ensuring complete stabilization of the mesh within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a needle holder to pull the long arms of the mesh out of the abdomen extraperitoneally, positioning the instrument two centimeters above the inguinal ligament within the cavity.
The surgeon pulls the mesh arm out of the abdominal cavity extraperitoneally and suspends it outside the abdomen using artery forceps, ensuring it is held at the correct depth on both sides.
Both arms of the mesh are visible outside the abdominal cavity before peritoneal closure, with the surgeon assessing the adequate tension required for the mesh arms.
The surgeon completes the extraperitonealization of the mesh, ensuring it is positioned outside the peritoneal cavity to prevent exposure to intra-abdominal contents.
The mesh is fully extraperitonealized, completing the hernia repair procedure.
